text: The surgeon prepares to convert a coiled sleeve into a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, focusing on the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
Patient is a fifty-year-old female with history of hypertension and obstructive sleep
She underwent a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy two years ago.
She did well after the surgery, tolerating diet normally.
Six months ago, she began to experience dysphagia to solids, tolerating only some liquids with constant daily vomiting.
Her BMI before sleeve was fifty-four.
Her actual BMI prior to the conversion is nineteen.
The upper GI fluoroscopy showed evident passage of contrast with two acute kinks on the sleeve, one superior and another inferior,
The video shows a proximal dilatation in the gastric sleeve, visible in the current imaging.
The esophagogastroduodenoscopy reveals a large proximal gastric dilatation with an acute angulation that is easily traversed by the endoscope.
The surgeon attempts to pass the scope at the level of the incisura, encountering an acute angulation that prevents successful passage despite multiple attempts.
Multiple trocars are visibly placed in the abdominal wall, arranged to facilitate access for the gastric bypass procedure.
During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes two acute angulations in the gastric tube, with visible adhesions maintaining the tube in a fixed position.
The surgeon begins the dissection at the hiatus using a monopolar hook, carefully separating tissue in this area.
The surgeon lyses all adhesions around the gastric tube, progressively freeing it from surrounding tissue.
After fully mobilizing the stomach, the surgeon dissects a posterior window around the gastric tube, ensuring complete circumferential access.
The surgeon uses two stapler loads to transect the stomach, visibly separating the gastric tube from the remaining stomach tissue.
The surgeon retracts the transverse colon to expose and identify the angle of the Treitz ligament, revealing its anatomical position in relation to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon measures fifty centimeters from the angle of Treitz along the jejunum and transects it using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon measures and runs the alimentary limb to a length of one hundred and twenty centimeters, preparing it for the next step in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon performs enterotomies using a monopolar hook, creating openings in the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis using a stapler, joining two sections of the gastrointestinal tract.
The surgeon over-sews the enterotomies in two layers using a 3-0 absorbable suture, ensuring secure closure of the openings created during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon begins the gastrojejunostomy by placing a continuous posterior outer layer of three-oh absorbable sutures, securing the connection between the stomach and jejunum.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform enterotomies, creating openings in the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon begins suturing a continuous inner-posterior layer using a 3-0 absorbable suture, following the completion of the outer layer in the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon begins suturing the inner-anterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy using three O absorbable sutures, following the completion of the posterior layer.
The surgeon begins a new suture from the opposite side of the gastrojejunostomy and joins it with the existing suture in the middle, enhancing the reconstruction of the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis by performing the outer anterior layer using absorbable sutures, finalizing the gastrojejunostomy.
The OR time was a hundred and twenty minutes.
The estimated blood loss, ten cc's.
No complications were registered and discharge was post-op day number one.
